cards.back: It returns true if the current expression is being evaluated at compile-time, and false if it's being evaluated at runtime.

cards.text: std::is_constant_evaluated() is defined in the {{c1::<type_traits>}} header and was introduced in {{c2::C++20}}.

cards.explanation: You can use std::is_constant_evaluated() to provide different implementations for compile-time vs runtime execution in the same function. This is useful when the compile-time implementation has restrictions that the runtime version doesn't.

cards.front: What is the purpose of the constinit specifier?
cards.back: constinit ensures that a variable is initialized at compile-time (like constexpr), but unlike constexpr, the variable is not const and can be modified at runtime.

cards.text: {{c1::constinit}} int x = 42; // initialized at {{c2::compile-time}}, but can be {{c3::modified at runtime}}

cards.front: What is the key difference between constexpr and constinit variables?
cards.back:
• constexpr: Initialized at compile-time AND is const (cannot be modified)
  • constinit: Initialized at compile-time BUT is not const (can be modified at runtime)

cards.question: Which of these variables can be modified after initialization?
cards.explanation: constexpr and const variables are immutable. constinit ensures compile-time initialization but does not make the variable const, so b can be modified at runtime.

cards.text: C++20 introduced {{c1::consteval}} functions and the {{c2::constinit}} specifier for compile-time initialization without const.

cards.front: When should you use constinit instead of constexpr?
cards.back: Use constinit when you want to ensure a global or static variable is initialized at compile-time (to avoid static initialization order fiasco) but still need to modify it at runtime.

cards.text: In C++23, you can use {{c1::if consteval}} as a shorthand for checking if code is executing at {{c2::compile-time}}.
{{c1::constexpr}} variables are const and cannot be modified, while {{c2::constinit}} variables can be {{c3::modified at runtime}} after compile-time initialization.
